coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots functionalized with hydrocarbons of different chain lengths
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2017 Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Vieira, Kayo Oliveira
Bettini, Jefferson
de Oliveira, Luiz Fernando Cappa
Ferrari, Jefferson Luis
Schiavon, Marco Antonio
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of novel multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and their covalent functionalization with amines by peptide bonds. The resulting CQDs consisted of quasi-spherical graphite nanocrystals around 10 nm diameter, which were capped by amines with different hydrocarbon chains such as propylamine, octylamine, dodecylamine and octadecylamine. The introduction of nitrogen atoms and the hydrocarbon chains in the surface of the CQDs dramatically affected their photoluminescence profiles, quantum yields and solubility. The photoluminescence emission wavelength of these novel organic-soluble CQDs depended on the excitation wavelength and their quantum yields varied with the chain length of the hydrocarbon chain attached to the surface of the carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanotubes
Photoluminescence
Quantum yield

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by CAPES, CNPq, and FAPEMIG. The authors would like to acknowledge the LME/LNNano for providing the equipment and technical support for experiments involving transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: NEW CARBON MATERIALS Volume 32, Issue 4, Jun 2017 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Received date: 22 Mar 2017; Revised date: 09 June 2017 *Corresponding author. E-mail: schiavon@ufsj.edu.br CopyrightÂ©2017, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-5805(17)60126-4 RESEARCH PAPER Synthesis of multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots functionalized with hydrocarbons of different chain lengths Kayo Oliveira Vieira1, Jefferson Bettini2, Luiz Fernando Cappa de Oliveira3, Jefferson Luis Ferrari1, Marco Antonio Schiavon1* 1 Grupo de Pesquisa em QuÃ­mica de Materiais â€“ (GPQM), Departamento de CiÃªncias Naturais, Universidade Federal de SÃ£o JoÃ£o del-Rei, Campus Dom Bosco, PraÃ§a Dom HelvÃ©cio, 74, CEP 36301-160, SÃ£o JoÃ£o del-Rei, MG, Brazil; 2 LaboratÃ³rio Nacional de Nanotecnologia, Centro Nacional de Pesquisa em Energia e Materiais, CEP 13083-970, Campinas - SP, Brazil; 3 NÃºcleo de Espectroscopia e Estrutura Molecular, Departamento de QuÃ­mica, Universidade Federal de Juiz de Fora, 36036-330 Juiz de Fora, MG, Brazil Abstract: We report the synthesis of novel multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and their covalent functionalization with amines by peptide bonds. The resulting CQDs consisted of quasi-spherical graphite nanocrystals around 10 nm diameter, which were capped by amines with different hydrocarbon chains such as propylamine, octylamine, dodecylamine and octadecylamine. The introduction of nitrogen atoms and the hydrocarbon chains in the surface of the CQDs dramatically affected their photoluminescence profiles, quantum yields and solubility. The photoluminescence emission wavelength of these novel organic-soluble CQDs depended on the excitation wavelength and their quantum yields varied with the chain length of the hydrocarbon chain attached to the surface of the carbon dots. Key Words: Carbon quantum dots; Carbon nanotubes; Photoluminescence; Quantum yield. 1 Introduction Amorphous carbon, graphite, and diamond were the first allotropic forms of carbon known to humanity [1] . However, the discovery of buckminsterfullerene (C60), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [2] , and graphene in recent decades has increased researchersâ€˜ interest in carbon nanostructures and has opened up exciting new fields in science and technology [1, 3] . Indeed, the scientific community has become enthusiastic about the potential applications of carbon nanostructures in energy conversion devices, biosensors, and drug delivery systems [1, 4, 5] . Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a young small member of the carbon nanomaterial family [6] . CQDs first emerged in 2004 during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) derived from arc-discharge soot by electrophoretic methods [7] . CQDs constitute a class of â€—zero-dimensional structures. They usually comprise quasi-spherical graphitic nanoparticles about 10 nm [8] . The nanoparticles consist of single-, bi-, or multi-layers of sp 2- hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a six-membered ring, which gives rise to a graphitic core. In addition, CQDs bear some functional groups on their surfaces [3, 6, 9] . CQDs offer unique advantages over traditional organic fluorescent agents and semiconducting quantum dots. They are cheap, chemically inert, easy to be functionalized, little toxic, and biocompatible. They display stable and tunable photoluminescence, and they can function as electron donors or acceptors [6, 10-16] . A number of techniques are used to prepare CQDs, including laser ablation [5] , pyrolysis [8] , electrochemical synthesis [17] , supported synthesis [18] , acid oxidation [12] , microwave-assisted synthesis [19,20] , and hydrothermal synthesis [21,23] . The synthetic approaches are classified as either â€•top-downâ€– or â€•bottom-upâ€– methods, depending on the precursors. Top-down methods refer to the preparation of CQDs from large carbon materials, such as carbon fibers [24] , graphite, and CNTs [25] , whereas bottom-up methods involve the synthesis of CQDs from molecular precursors such as citric acid, glucose, and organic resins [8, 20, 21, 26] . For several years, oxidization by mixed acids has been the standard method to break down and functionalize carbon nanomaterials [25, 27-29] . Many works have used materials such as carbon fibers, graphite, and CNTs as precursors of CQDs [17, 24, 25, 29, 30] . Recently, researchers have employed oxidization of SWCNTs, [30] multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 graphite [25] by mixed acids to obtain CQDs with similar sizes, morphologies, and optical properties. Nitrogen-doped nanostructured carbon materials have also attracted much attention since such doping efficiently induces charge delocalization and â€—tunesâ€˜ the carbon work function [16] . CQDs post-passivated with functional amine groups (NCQDs) exhibit significantly enhanced photoluminescence as compared with the original CQDs [10] . The introduction of functional amine groups into CQDs is another effective way to modulate their emission color and efficiency [10, 16, 29] . This study reports on a simple and reproducible route to synthesize NCQDs via MWCNT destruction. More specifically, we applied a top-down method that comprised three steps: (1) MWCNT oxidation by acid attack to obtain â€”C(O)OH groups; (2) treatment of the resulting product with SOCl2, to generate â€”C(O)Cl groups; and (3) conversion of the â€”C(O)Cl groups to â€”C(O)NR by reaction with amine. This route produced CQDs functionalized with amines via formation of peptidic bonds. We attached carbon chains with different lengths to the sp 2 structure of CQDs by reacting the â€”C(O)Cl groups with amines such as propylamine, octylamine, dodecylamine, and octadecylamine. Then the morphological and optical properties of the final CQDs were characterized. 2 Experimental 2.1 Synthesis of CQDs MWCNTs (4.0 g, 95% purity, external diameter ranging from 10 to 40 nm, and length varying between 5 and 20 um) were purchased from CNT Co. Ltd, Incheon, Korea, and added to a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (300 mL) and HNO3 (100 mL). The resulting solution was sonicated for 2 h, which was followed by reflux at 80 Â± 3 Â°C under constant stirring for 8 h. A light brown gas evolved. [27] After the solution was cooled and centrifuged, its pH was adjusted to 8.0 with a solution of NaOH. The product was washed four or five times with distilled water, dried in a vacuum oven at 70 Â°C, and identified as carboxylated MWCNTs. Reflux in excess of SOCl2 (25 mL for 0.1 g) and dimethylformamide (DMF) (1 mL) at 70â€“72 Â°C for 24 h converted the carboxyl groups of the carboxylated MWCNTs to carbonyl chloride groups. The product was separated by distillation, dried in a vacuum oven at 70 Â°C, and identified as carbonyl chloride CQDs (ClCQDs). The ClCQDs were amidated by reaction with excess propylamine (PPA), octylamine (OCT), dodecylamine (DDA), or octadecylamine (ODA) in liquid phase. The products consisted of nitrogen-containing CQDs (NCQDs) functionalized with carbon chains containing 3 (from propylamine PPA), 8 (from octylamine OCT), 12 (from dodecylamine DDA), and 18 (from octadecylamine ODA) carbon atoms. Multiple extractions with methanol (four times with 50 mL portions) separated the NCQDs from the excess amine used during the amidation reaction. 50 mL of hexane made the organic material soluble. The CQDs functionalized with amines were filtered and centrifuged at 3600 rpm for 1 h, NCQDs stay in the supernatant and the precipitate was discarded. 2.2 Photoluminescence quantum yield measurements The following equation helped to calculate the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm: QYx = QYST (mx / mST) (Î· 2 x /Î· 2 ST ) where the subscripts ST and X denote standard and test samples, respectively, QYST is the photoluminescence quantum yield of the standard, m refers to the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and Î· corresponds to the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate (QY of 54% in 0.1 mol L -1 H2SO4 solution) served as a reference. [21, 31] 2.3 Characterization Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectra were registered on a diode array UV-2550 Shimadzu spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a 150-W xenon lamp at room temperature. The PL decay curves were recorded by a spectroï¬‚uorimeter (Horiba-Jobin Yvon Fluorolog 3, Horiba Scientific, New Jersey, USA) using nanoled of the 340 nm. Absorption and fluorescence were measured in quartz cuvettes with 10-mm optical path (Shimadzu). For this purpose, air-saturated solutions were used at room temperature.The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on a Perkin Elmer spectrometer (Spectrum GX) operating in the transmission mode. 32 spectra with 2 cm -1 resolution were collected. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai-G2-20 and JEM 2100 FEG-TEM microscope operating at 200 kV. Liquid samples were dispersed in hexane and placed on an ultrathin carbon film substrate supported by a copper grid. Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Fig 1 (a) Scheme for the three-step synthesis of amidated CQDs. TEM images of (b) MWCNTs and (c) NCQDs. (d) HRTEM images of NCQDs. They were left open for the solvent to evaporate. Raman spectra were obtained on a confocal Raman Scope III microscope coupled to a Bruker RFS-100 spectrometer equipped with a Nd:YAG laser (excitation wavelength = 1 064 nm) and operating in the backscattering configuration with a resolution of 4 cm -1 . 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Synthetic strategy We decided to use MWCNTs as the precursor material on the basis of literature reports [25, 27-30, 32, 33] . It is easier to destroy the 1-D structure of MWCNTs than to deconstruct the structure of graphite. The curvature induces pyramidalization and misalignment of the Ï€ orbitals of the carbon atoms in MWCNTs induce local strain, so CNTs should be more reactive than a flat graphene sheet [32] . Compared with CQDs synthesized from molecular precursors via bottom-up methods, MWCNTs are also advantageous in that they yield highly crystalline nanoparticles bearing sp 2- structured cores [8] , which should provide information about how functional groups affect the photoluminescence properties of CQDs. 3.2 Formation mechanism and morphological an Fig 1. (a) Scheme for the three-step synthesis of amidated CQDs. TEM images of (b) MWCNTs and (c) NCQDs. (d) HRTEM images of NCQDs. Fig. 1a depicts the synthesis and functionalization of CQDs. The main approach to functionalizing MWCNTs starts with carboxylation via an acid treatment. [25, 27-29, 32] Heating of MWCNTs in a mixture of concentrated HNO3 and H2SO4 at a 1:3 ratio (v/v) functionalizes the CNTs and partially destroys their sp 2 structure (Step 1). The TEM images in Fig. S1a show carboxylated MWCNTs and carboxylated graphene sheets. The MWCNTs seem to open along a line, in a process that resembles the â€—unzippingâ€˜ of graphite oxide. This could happen either as a linear longitudinal cut or in a spiraling fashion, depending on the initial site of acid attack and the chiral angle of the CNT. [33] The carboxylic acid functional groups (â€”COOH) generated on the surface of CNTs constitute the prototype chemical group for surface functionalization. The carbonyl carbon atom covalently binds to the tube and enables exchange of the â€“OH group with other groups via standard chemical reactions, with consequent attachment of more complex molecules. [27, 28, 32] Reaction with thionyl chloride (SOCl2) (Step 2) converts the grafted carboxyl groups, â€“C(O)OH, to carbonyl chloride groups, â€“ C(O)Cl. Fig. S2 illustrates the reaction mechanism. The acidic conditions destroy the remaining MWCNTs, giving rise to clusters of chlorine-containing CQDs (ClCQDs). Fig. S1b displays the TEM images of ClCQDs. Finally, reaction of ClCQDs with excess molten amine readily produces NCQDs (Fig. 1, Step 3), which are well dispersed and have similar size. Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 The TEM image in Fig. 1d shows that the CQDs are homogeneous and have an average diameter of about 10 nm. These results are similar to other studies that used acid oxidation and reaction with SOCl2 to obtain CQDs [29] . The CQDs prepared herein have a uniform crystalline structure consisting of parallel crystal planes. This structure resembles the structure of the multiple walls of the MWCNTs before destruction. The image of the CQDs reveals a high crystallinity and lattice spacing of 0.32 nm. These findings agree with data reported for nanoparticles derived from CNTs and graphite [5,14,17,32,34] . The CQDs originated from MWCNTs, so they must exhibit the same uniformity and purity as the starting material. The synthesis of NCQDs via MWCNT destruction has a low yield, below the 1.0 wt% of the starting mass of the MWCNTs. This low yield is due to the weight loses after several purification processes, when a high concentration of acids and anime in the synthesis steps had been used as described above. Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to characterize crystalline, nanocrystalline, and amorphous carbons [35] . The great versatility of carbon materials arises from the strong dependence of their physical properties on the ratio between sp 2 and sp 3 bonds [35,36] . In the present work, Raman spectroscopy aided the investigation on the degree of disorder in MWCNTs and CQDs. Fig. 2 shows the Raman spectra of the starting MWCNTs, carboxylated MWCNTs, and CQDs. All the spectra display the typical D and G bands at 1 322 and 1 570 cm -1 , respectively. The D band around 1 322 cm -1 is a breathing mode of the A1g symmetry and involves phonons. This mode is forbidden in perfect graphite and only becomes active in the presence of disorder. [5, 35, 36] The G band at 1 570 cm -1 refers to a graphite vibration that involves the in-plane bond-stretching motion of sp 2 -bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2-D) hexagonal lattice with the E2g symmetry. [5, 35, 36] The ratio between the intensities of the G and D bands is a commonly accepted indicator of sample crystallinity. Here, this ratio suggests that the graphite-like structure of the prepared CQDs is partially disordered. [36, 37] The ratio between the D and G band areas (ID/IG) quantifies the degree of defect in graphite materials submitted to surface modification. It is possible to determine the ID/IG ratios for MWCNTs, carboxylated MWCNTs (Fig. 2a), and ClCQDs (Fig. 2b) by adjusting the spectra via Lorentzian curves and then integrating the G and D bands (Fig. S4). In the case of MWCNTs, carboxylated MWCNTs, and ClCQDs, this procedure affords ID/IG ratios of 1.14, 1.51, and 1.77, respectively. Increasing ID/IG ratios denote higher disorder and larger sp 3 /sp 2 carbon ratio, which implies that functionalization introduces structural defects into the nanoparticles. The extension of the band in the spectrum of the ClCQDs is due to formation of graphite nanocrystals [35] . Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of the samples NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, NCQDsâ€”OCT, and NCQDsâ€”PPA. They evidence broadening of the band corresponding to the sp 2 carbon, the G band (with extension of the G band being due to the formation of graphite nanocrystals), deformation in the sp 2 structure, clustering of the sp 2 phase, and introduction of carbon sp 3 hybridization from the hydrocarbon chains of the amines used to functionalize the NCQDs [35] . Attempts to adjust the spectra via Lorentzian curves followed by integration of the G and D bands failed in the case of these samples. Unmodified CNTs do not form stable dispersions in water, so dispersion of the carboxylated MWCNTs in water helps the MWCNT functionalization with carboxylate groups. Fig. S4 presents images of the dispersion of MWCNTs and carboxylated MWCNTs in water. FTIR helped to identify the organic functional groups on the surface of the MWCNTs and CQDs in all the synthetic steps. The carboxylated MWCNTs (Fig. 4a) exhibit a strong band between 3 420 and 3 460 cm â€“1 due to bound -OH groups. The bands at 2 922 and 2 854 cm â€“1 refer to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of the -CH2- groups. The band at 1 710 cm -1 is due to the carbonyl function of carboxylic acid. The FTIR spectrum of ClCQDs in Fig 4a presents a doublet peak at 1 705â€“1 614 cm â€“1 , characteristic of C=O vibrations in the carbonyl chloride group. To confirm acylation, we reacted the ClCQDs with water, to regenerate the carboxylated CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of the resulting sample (Fig. S6) resembled the spectrum of carboxylated MWCNTs in Fig. 4a. The little intense band at 1 710 cm -1 confirmed the presence of carbonyl from carboxylic acid. Fig. 4b depicts the FTIR spectra of NCQDs bearing hydrocarbon substituents with different chain lengths. The band at 1 650 cm -1 , attributed to C=O stretching of amides, and the bands at 1 573 and 1 250 cm -1 , ascribed to stretching of Nâ€”H and Câ€”N bonds, respectively, confirm that hydrocarbon chains exist in the amide groups present on the surface of the NCQDs. Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Fig. 2 (a) Raman spectra of MWCNTs and carboxylated MWCNTs. (b) Lorentzian fit and spectrum simulated with the fit (red line) of the D and G bands of ClCQDs. Fig. 3 Raman spectra of NCQDs with hydrocarbon substituents bearing different chain lengths. 3.3 Absorbance and luminescence properties The insets in Fig. 4 reveal that CQDs in hexane and aqueous dispersions are yellowish and transparent in daylight, but they exhibit bright blue luminescence under excitation with 355-nm UV light. The samples NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, and NCQDsâ€”OCT, dispersed in hexane, and the samples NCQDsâ€”PPT and carboxylated CQDs, dispersed in water, display similar broad absorption in the UV-vis range. The QCDs have a maximum absorption at 275 nm, with a tail extending into the visible range. This is assigned to the n-Ï€* transition of the C=O band and to the Ï€-Ï€* transition of the conjugated C=C band of an aromatic p orbital system, like the orbital system of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [4, 5, 11, 22, 30, 38] . CNTs can display metallic properties [39] , and graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor, which limits its electronic and optoelectronic applications. The lack of a band gap prevents graphene and MWCNTs from displaying optical luminescence [24] . Indeed, the carboxylated MWCNTs and graphene sheets obtained in Step 1 do not possess optical luminescence. Fig. 6 contains the photoluminescence spectra of the prepared CQDs measured at a fixed excitation wavelength of 355 nm. All the CQDs present visible photoluminescence with emission maxima centered at 442, 432, 425, 461, and 449 nm for NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, NCQDsâ€”OCT, NCQDsâ€”PPA, and carboxylated CQDs, respectively. Compared with carboxylated CQDs, the emission peaks of NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, and NCQDsâ€”OCT shift toward the blue, whereas the emission band of NCQDsâ€”PPA shifts toward the red. All the samples functionalized with a primary amine display an extension of the emission band to higher wavelengths. Size effects and surface states in CQDs control the photoluminescence mechanism. The fluorescence emission of CQDs results from their polyaromatic sp 2 carbon nanostructures and the various surface functional groups present in the nanoparticles [25] . These functional groups may lead to a series of surface states that usually act as charge carrier emissive traps between Câ€“C bonds and Ï€-Ï€* [11] . Qian et al. examined how surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots affects photoluminescence [29] . Comparison of the band gaps in the original polyaromatic structures with the band gaps in the amine-functionalized structures reveals that surface functionalization with amines remarkably widens the band gap. Therefore, the surface can also tune the fluorescence of CQDs. Emission from these states is always red-shifted as compared with emission originating from the core [40] . The photoluminescence of carboxylated CQDs resembles the photoluminescence of CQDs obtained via destruction of MWCNTs by acid attack [25] . Hence, the change in surface groups impacts not only the size and solubility of CQDs, but also their optical properties [40] . The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior of NCQDs (Fig. 7) verified in this study agrees with previous reports [11, 25, 30, 38, 40] . This behavior likely stems from surface states rather than the size of aromatic domains in CQDs-we used the same method to synthesize all the NCQDs and they all have the same size distribution. Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Fig. 4 FTIR spectra of (a) MWCNTs, carboxylated MWCNTs, and ClCQDs, and (b) NCQDs bearing amides with different hydrocarbon chain lengths. Fig. 5 UV-vis spectra of carboxylated CQDs and NCQDs bearing hydrocarbon substituents with different chain lengths dispersed in hexane. Inset: CQDs are yellowish transparent in daylight and exhibit blue luminescence under excitation with 355-nm UV light. The photoluminescence emission of NCQDs using excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 600 nm shows that the emission maxima shift toward the red with the increment in the excitation wavelength (Fig. 7). Compared with NCQDs, carboxylated CQDs have a slightly red-shifted maximum wavelength. All the studied samples display a maximum emission upon excitation at 350 nm, except for NCQDsâ€”PPA, which presents a maximum emission upon excitation at 600 nm. This is an important feature for in vivo optical imaging, where longer wavelengths are usually preferred, because photons exhibit an improved tissue penetration, and background autofluorescence remains minimal, especially in the NIR region. The potential application of NCQDsâ€”PPA in in vivo fluorescence imaging (excitation = 700 nm, emission = 730 nm) suggests their promising use as optical nanoprobes in biomedical imaging [25] . NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, and NCQDsâ€”OCT behave similarly upon excitation at different wavelengths. However, it is clear that the longer the hydrocarbon chain bound to NCQDs, the more the photoluminescence intensity shifts to longer wavelengths. These results highlight that the Fig. 6 Photoluminescence emission spectra of NCQDsâ€”DDA, NQCDsâ€”OCT, and NQCDsâ€”ODA dispersed in hexane and of carboxylated CQDs and NCQDsâ€”PPA dispersed in water (Î»ex = 355 nm). length of the hydrocarbon chains improves the photoluminescence properties of NCQDs. Emission spectra of the CQDs were dependent on excitation wavelength. The shift in emission peak positions with different excitation wavelengths arose not only from different sizes of C-dots, but also primarily from different emissive trap sites on CQDs surface. It is reported in the literature that surface groups, such as C=O, Câ€”O, O=Câ€”OH, and amino groups can introduce trapping states with different energy levels between HOMO and LUMO. Therefore, under certain excitation wavelengths, some corresponding emissive sites would be excited resulting in CQDs that emit light [29, 41, 42] . The photographs of the samples under different excitation wavelengths in Fig. 8 evidence the red shift in wavelength. To gain a more insight into the photoluminescence properties of NCQDs, we determined the photoluminescence quantum yield using quinine sulfate as a reference. NCQDsâ€”ODA, NCQDsâ€”DDA, and NCQDsâ€”OCT give rise to a dispersion in organic solvents, Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Fig. 7 Fluorescence spectra of (a) carboxylated CQDs, (b) NCQDsâ€”ODA, (c) NCQDsâ€”DDA, (d) NCQDsâ€”OCT, and (e) NCQDsâ€”PPA under various excitation wavelengths. (f) Structures of the amines used to functionalize CQDs. Fig.8 Photographs of NCQDs under ultraviolet, blue, green, and red light excitation. (a) NCQDsâ€”ODA, (b) NCQDsâ€”DDA, (c) NCQDsâ€”OCT and (d) NCQDsâ€”PPA. whereas carboxylated CQDs and NCQDsâ€“PPA are water-soluble. Table 1 lists the photoluminescence quantum yields for the studied samples. Although the QY values are quite low, they are based on a quinine sulfate standard, which is suitable for this type of measurement. It is not possible to compare many QY literature results because there is no information on the quality of the experimental procedure used to obtain the QY values. In addition, most data refer to experimental sets that use dyes with a higher QY, such as rhodamine. Fig. S7 to S11 present the linear fit that we employed for the CQDs and quinone sulfate as well as for determination of QY. Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 Fig. 9 The photoluminescence lifetimes curves of the different CQDs and the equation used for exponential fitting. Table 1 Photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) for carboxylated CQDs and NCQDs bearing hydrocarbon substituents with different chain lengths. The only difference between carboxylated CQDs and NCQDs lies on the surface functional group, which implies that the amino group has a specific function in enhancing the fluorescence of CQDs. This is consistent with the remarkably elevated quantum yields of CQDs functionalized with amino groups reported in previous papers [10, 16, 29] . The amino functional group has a specific function to enhance the fluorescence of CQDs, which was consistent with previous papers, in which the quantum yields of CQDs functionalized with amino groups were elevated [10, 16, 29] . The amino-groups has more valence electrons than carbon that acts as an electron-donor, as a result, the probability of electron transition from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state (and thus â€“ optical absorption) should be higher [41] . We found that the QY of carboxylated CQDs is 0.5%, whilst the QY of the investigated NCQDs varies from 0.4% to 5.0%. The QY results summarized in Table I indicate that the QY values depend on the chain length of the hydrocarbon substituent in the NCQDs since larger chains such as OCT, DDA, and ODA lead to higher QY (5%, 3% and 0.4% for NCQDsâ€”OCT, NCQDsâ€”DDA, and NCQDsâ€”ODA, respectively). Paradoxically, functionalization of NCQDs with the short-chain PPA gives a QY value of 1%. The 50% higher QY obtained for NCQDsâ€”PPA as compared with carboxylated CQDs probably results from enhanced surface passivation promoted by the attached modifier. The beneficial effect of the hydrocarbon chain on the QY value of NCQDs is more evident for NCQDsâ€”OCT than for NCQDs with larger hydrocarbon chain lengths (NCQDsâ€”DDA and NCQDsâ€”ODA). Thus, the change in surface groups markedly affects not only the photoluminescence and solubility of CQDs, but also their photoluminescence quantum yield. Despite the low quantum yield values of the CQDs in relation to other studies that showed values above 50%. [43, 44] The procedure for the determination of photoluminescence quantum yields with optical methods requires a series of the recommendations on the use of standards for the measurement of photoluminescence QY. Many of the compounds that have been proposed as QY standards are fluorescing dyes. A suitable QY standard should absorb and emit within similar spectral regions as the sample. Also, the standard should be excitable at an excitation wavelength suitable for the sample [31, 45] . We calculate the QY following the methodology recommended by IUPAC, using the standard fitting and present the curves obtained in supplementary information, what makes them more reliable results compared to other studies which present only the QY values, and do not describe about the methodology used to find them. The QY values we have found could be judged as low if compared with semiconductor nanocrystals QY values, which generally give values up to 50%-80%. However, for CQDs the obtained results can be considered quite good if we take in account that the material is based on carbon, and that the same method used to determine the QY of semiconductor nanocrystals, which is already well-know in literature. Most of QY results for CQDs presented in literature, which claims higher values, compared to semiconductor nanocrystals, do not present any details of how their results were acquired. So, this is why we have shown our analytical curves to fundament our results (Fig. S7 to S11). The structural properties that influence the photoluminescence behavior of CQDs are the size of the graphite core, the bounded functional groups and the size of the carbon chain on the surface of the nanoparticles. The band gap diminishes with increasing the size of the graphite core, as already observed in other works. [10, 25, 29] The size of the CQDs can be experimentally controlled by the centrifugation at 3600 RPM during 1 hour. This procedure reduces the effect of size distribution in the photoluminescent properties, once all samples are with the same size distribution. The emission can be tunable by changing the bounded functional groups, as shown in Fig. 7. The carboxylated CQDs showed the blue emission and NCQDs showed a red emission, the amine functional group leads to the formation of surface states with lower energy making them photoluminescent at higher wavelength [25, 29] . Besides, the amino-groups lead to higher values of the photoluminescence quantum yields. However, the length of carbon chain of the amines has the influence on Sample QY (%) Carboxylated CQDs 0.5 NCQDsâ€”PPA 1.0 NCQDsâ€”OCT 5.0 NCQDsâ€”DDA 3.0 NCQDsâ€”ODA 0.4 Kayo Oliveira Vieira et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2017, 32(4): 327-337 the quantum yield, the bigger is the size of the chain of the amines, the lower is the value of photoluminescence quantum yield, except for NCQDsâ€”PPA which presented a quantum yield value lower than the NCQDsâ€”OCT. This exception can be explained by the fact the synthesis was carried out in water, which may have led to this change of trend. The photoluminescence lifetimes of the different CQDs were obtained by analysis of the photoluminescence decays. The lifetime data of CQDs were calculated with a three-component decay time model, they were very well fitted with a triple exponential function as shown in Fig. 9. The observed lifetimes observed for the carboxylated CQDs are t1 =1.3, t2= 1.3 and t 3= 6.1 ns, NCQDsâ€”PPA are t 1= 1.9, t 2=1.9 ns and t3 =7.2 ns, NCQDsâ€”OCT are t1=2.1 t2=2.1 are t3=8.3 ns, NCQDsâ€”DDA are t1=1.9, t2=7.6 and t3=1.9 ns, and NCQDsâ€”ODA are t1=26.7 t2=7.4 and t3=2.1 ns. The multiple photoluminescence lifetimes of the CQDs imply various emission species, which may be a wide range of chemical environments on the surface of carbon dots and aromatic carbonyls of the core as well. 4 Conclusions We have described a simple, top-down method to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different functional groups from MWCNTs. The obtained CQDs consisted of spherical graphite nanocrystals (measuring 10 nm on average) capped with different hydrocarbon substituents via peptide bonds. The CQDs absorbed in the UV region, but absorption extended into the visible range. The length of the surface groups dramatically affected the photoluminescence quantum yield and solubility of CQDs. Functionalization of CQDs with amines bearing 18, 12, and 8 carbon atoms shifted the fluorescence toward the blue. The fluorescence of CQDs functionalized with propylamine was red-shifted. All the samples functionalized with amine presented an emission band that extended into higher wavelengths, because amine functionalization increased the quantum yield. The chain length of the hydrocarbon substituent influenced how the CQDs can be dispersed in solvents. The samples functionalized with amines containing 18, 12 and, 8 carbon atoms dispersed well in organic solvents, whereas carboxylated CQDs and CQDs functionalized with amines bearing 3 carbon atoms were water-soluble. Acknowledgements This work was supported by CAPES, CNPq, and FAPEMIG. The authors would like to acknowledge the LME/LNNano for providing the equipment and technical support for experiments involving transmission electron microscopy (TEM). ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots functionalized with hydrocarbons of different chain lengths

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Grupo de Pesquisa em Química de Materiais – (GPQM), Departamento de Ciências Naturais, Universidade Federal de São João del-Rei, Campus Dom Bosco, Praça Dom Helvécio, 74, CEP 36301-160, São João del-Rei, MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Grupo de Pesquisa em Química de Materiais – (GPQM)
Universidade Federal de São João del-Rei

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratório Nacional de Nanotecnologia, Centro Nacional de Pesquisa em Energia e Materiais, CEP 13083-970, Campinas – SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro Nacional de Pesquisa em Energia e Materiais

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Núcleo de Espectroscopia e Estrutura Molecular, Departamento de Química, Universidade Federal de Juiz de Fora, 36036-330 Juiz de Fora, MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Núcleo de Espectroscopia e Estrutura Molecular
Universidade Federal de Juiz de Fora

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of novel multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and their covalent functionalization with amines by peptide bonds. The resulting CQDs consisted of quasi-spherical graphite nanocrystals around 10 nm diameter, which were capped by amines with different hydrocarbon chains such as propylamine, octylamine, dodecylamine and octadecylamine. The introduction of nitrogen atoms and the hydrocarbon chains in the surface of the CQDs dramatically affected their photoluminescence profiles, quantum yields and solubility. The photoluminescence emission wavelength of these novel organic-soluble CQDs depended on the excitation wavelength and their quantum yields varied with the chain length of the hydrocarbon chain attached to the surface of the carbon dots.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanomaterials for advanced energy conversion and storage[J]
